skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, read, edit, analyze, and format Excel workbooks with Python. The skill covers openpyxl and pandas workflows for formulas, charts, worksheets, CSV conversion, and reusable spreadsheet helpers.
skill.summary: Build and analyze Excel workbooks with formulas, charts, formatting, and pandas integration.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the C2, weak crypto, reconnaissance, dynamic import, and backtick alerts are false positives from Excel cell references, Markdown code fences, normal Python imports, and documentation links. The confirmed risk is moderate because the helper scripts read and write local files and can place formulas into workbooks without sanitizing untrusted spreadsheet or CSV values. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration behavior was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Formula Injection Exposure in Spreadsheet Output
medium_findings.description: The helper writes values and DataFrames directly to Excel files and intentionally supports formulas. If untrusted CSV or user values begin with formula characters, opening the workbook could execute spreadsheet formulas. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic issue missed by static analysis.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly writes raw values and DataFrames to workbooks and documents CSV conversion. The risk depends on whether inputs are trusted, so confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick and external command alerts occur in Markdown fences or installation instructions, not Ruby execution paths. Examples show Python workbook code or benign pip and uv commands. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for the static findings in README.md, SKILL.md, examples, and references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows fenced Markdown examples and package installation commands. I found no Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation in the Python scripts.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Spreadsheet Cell References
low_findings.description: The high C2 alerts are Excel cell references and ranges, such as ws['C2'] and C2:C100. The weak crypto alerts do not correspond to cryptographic APIs or hash use in the reviewed code. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are spreadsheet coordinates or examples. Import review found no md5, sha1, crypto library use, command-and-control behavior, or reconnaissance intent.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Do Not Create Runtime Network Access
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to official openpyxl, pandas, Microsoft Excel, and chart documentation. They are resource links, not HTTP requests in executable code. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for network execution risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown external links only. No requests, urllib, socket, or similar network code appeared in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alerts Are Normal Python Import Examples
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts refer to standard Python import statements in an example script and documentation snippet. They do not compute module names from user input. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports are static helper imports with explicit symbols. There is no importlib, __import__, eval, or dynamic module expression at these locations.

low_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Requires User Awareness
low_findings.description: The helper reads input paths and writes output workbooks by design. This is expected for an Excel skill, but users should avoid running it on sensitive files without review. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE low operational risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and useful for the skill purpose. Risk comes from broad local file access if the agent is allowed to choose paths unsafely.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsanitized Spreadsheet Formula and Value Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can write formulas and raw values into Excel workbooks. Treat untrusted CSV, workbook, and prompt-provided cell values as unsafe unless formula-leading values are escaped or validated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct workbook writes are confirmed. Exploitability depends on untrusted input handling and downstream workbook opening behavior.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet automation is slow when formulas, charts, and formatting must be built by hand. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create reliable Excel workflows with Python helpers and practical examples.
actual_capabilities: Creates Excel workbooks with worksheets, headers, formulas, and styled cells.
Reads existing .xlsx and .xlsm files with openpyxl or pandas.
Writes pandas DataFrames to Excel with optional header formatting.
Builds line, bar, pie, scatter, and area charts from worksheet ranges.
Applies conditional formatting, dropdown validation, numeric validation, and date validation.
Provides examples for dashboards, financial reports, CSV transformation, and multi-sheet workbooks.
limitations: It does not evaluate Excel formulas inside Python.
It does not support legacy .xls editing with openpyxl.
It needs Python packages such as openpyxl and pandas installed.
Untrusted spreadsheet values need review before formula-capable export.

use_cases.title: Monthly Finance Report
use_cases.description: Create formatted income statements with totals, currency formats, borders, and reusable formulas.

use_cases.title: CSV Data Conversion
use_cases.description: Transform raw CSV exports into clean Excel workbooks with summaries and charts.

use_cases.title: Reusable Spreadsheet Template
use_cases.description: Build workbook templates with validation rules, headers, formulas, and presentation-ready formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the xlsx skill to create an Excel workbook with one sheet, headers, sample rows, totals, and basic formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Format an Existing Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the xlsx skill to update my workbook while preserving formulas. Add header styling, currency formats, and adjusted column widths.

prompt_templates.title: Convert CSV to Excel Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the xlsx skill to convert this CSV into an Excel report with a summary sheet, grouped totals, and a chart.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Sheet Dashboard
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the xlsx skill to create a multi-sheet dashboard with source data, KPI formulas, validation rules, and multiple charts.

output_examples.input: Create a quarterly sales workbook for three products.
output_examples.output: A workbook with a sales sheet, product rows, quarterly columns, total formulas, styled headers, currency formatting, and adjusted column widths.

output_examples.input: Turn a sales CSV into an Excel summary report.
output_examples.output: An Excel file with raw data, grouped revenue summaries, top product analysis, and a chart showing sales by product.

output_examples.input: Build a dashboard for monthly performance.
output_examples.output: A dashboard sheet with KPI metrics, trend charts, profit calculations, professional colors, and a refresh timestamp.

best_practices: Review untrusted CSV and workbook values before writing formula-capable Excel files.
Use read-only or write-only workbook modes for large files.
Load workbooks without data_only when formulas must be preserved.
anti_patterns: Do not overwrite a formula workbook after loading it with data_only enabled.
Do not export untrusted text that starts with formula characters without escaping it.
Do not use cell-by-cell loops for very large datasets when bulk writes are possible.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Excel files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples and helpers for creating workbooks, sheets, rows, formulas, formatting, and charts.

faq.question: Can it edit existing workbooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses openpyxl to load and modify existing .xlsx and .xlsm files while preserving formulas when used correctly.

faq.question: Does it support CSV files?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents pandas workflows for reading CSV data and exporting formatted Excel reports.

faq.question: Can it calculate formula results?
faq.answer: No. It writes and preserves formulas, but Excel or another spreadsheet engine calculates the results.

faq.question: What packages does it use?
faq.answer: The main packages are openpyxl for workbook manipulation and pandas for data analysis and tabular export.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted spreadsheets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review formulas, links, and values from untrusted files before exporting or opening generated workbooks.
